patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a cooperative, clear-speaking 9-year-old brought by a parent. Start by saying: “I keep getting painful skin boils that come back, and I’ve had lots of chest infections with cough and thick mucus.” Volunteer that you’ve needed antibiotics many times but infections return, and you’ve had itchy, dry, rashy skin since little.
If asked, add: boils can drain pus and scar; fevers/chills with flares; frequent sinus infections with facial pressure and thick drainage; more ear infections than friends; wheeze/shortness of breath when sick; thrush/white mouth patches after antibiotics.
Do NOT mention unless specifically asked about growth, teeth, labs, or minor wounds: you’re smaller than siblings, lost baby teeth late/needed teeth pulled, small cuts/bug bites get infected, and you were told IgE/allergy numbers are very high.
Be embarrassed and hesitant about boils in private areas—only admit if asked gently.
Mention misconceptions: maybe eczema/soap allergy, MRSA from sharing towels, or “asthma.”
If clinician is empathetic, relax and answer fully. Express family worry about too many tests and cost; admit you sometimes skip/space antibiotics because stomach upset and to make them last.

comorbidities: Recurrent skin and soft tissue infections (history of Staphylococcus aureus abscesses)

confounders: Atopic dermatitis with secondary impetiginization can mimic primary infectious burden
Mild persistent asthma with recurrent cough/wheeze can mask early pneumonia

Explains treatment expectations: no proven curative antiviral for Japanese encephalitis; management is supportive care, often in the ICU (airway/ventilation, fluids/electrolytes, fever control, seizure management, ICP management).

Performs critical safety checks before actions: verifies contraindications before ordering/initiating any high-risk interventions (e.g., lumbar puncture in suspected elevated ICP/coagulopathy) and completes an allergy check before prescribing any medications (critical).
Performs a drug interaction check before prescribing when feasible (non-critical) and adjusts plan if interactions are identified.

Includes empiric coverage for alternative treatable causes of encephalitis/meningitis when clinically indicated (e.g., acyclovir for HSV encephalitis; antibiotics if bacterial meningitis cannot be excluded) while awaiting confirmation.

Elicits key history elements relevant to Japanese encephalitis risk: recent travel/residence in endemic areas, rural/agricultural exposure, mosquito exposure, seasonality, vaccination status, and incubation timeframe.
Assesses and documents severity/red flags: GCS/mental status, seizure activity, focal neurologic deficits, neck stiffness, vital signs, and airway/breathing status.

Discusses prognosis and potential neurologic sequelae, sets expectations about recovery, and provides prevention counseling (mosquito avoidance, vaccination for future risk) without delaying acute care.
Uses clear, empathetic communication and checks understanding; avoids false reassurance and avoids claiming antibiotics/antivirals definitively cure Japanese encephalitis.

Uses clear, patient-appropriate language to explain what PPoma is (a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor subtype) and why it can be associated with obstructive jaundice/cholestasis (e.g., mass effect/biliary obstruction).

Acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty appropriately and documents at least one reasonable alternative diagnosis if data are incomplete (e.g., pancreatic adenocarcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, gallstone obstruction), without contradicting the final recorded diagnosis.
Does not prescribe medications or place orders in an L1 diagnosis-only task; focuses on diagnostic reasoning and next diagnostic steps in narrative form only.

Mentions a safe plan for urgent escalation if red flags are present or develop (e.g., fever/rigors, hypotension, confusion suggesting ascending cholangitis; severe abdominal pain; worsening jaundice).
If discussing future treatment, includes both "octreotide" and "ERCP" as potential management elements, while clarifying they are contingent on confirmation and clinical context.

Demonstrates awareness of required safety checks conceptually: before any future prescribing, would verify allergies; before ordering invasive procedures/contrast/sedation-related interventions, would check contraindications; and would consider drug interactions when relevant.

minimal_paths.description: Identify PPoma as the most likely diagnosis explaining cholestasis and communicate it clearly, with a safe, structured diagnostic rationale.
must_collect: right upper quadrant or epigastric pain
history of gallstones or prior biliary disease
family history of endocrine tumors/MEN1 features
prior imaging or labs if available (bilirubin pattern, ALP/GGT)
steps: Elicit symptoms consistent with cholestasis and screen for cholangitis red flags.
Assess risk factors for pancreaticobiliary malignancy and neuroendocrine tumor syndromes (including MEN1 features if relevant).
Synthesize findings to explain that PPoma (pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor) can present with cholestasis via biliary obstruction/mass effect.
Communicate diagnosis to the patient including both required terms: PPoma and cholestasis.

Outline next diagnostic confirmation steps in narrative form (e.g., cross-sectional imaging and tumor markers as appropriate) and safety-net instructions for urgent care if cholangitis symptoms occur.
Document the diagnosis using the record_diagnosis tool.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis-first with explicit differential before final diagnosis; still records PPoma as working diagnosis and explains cholestasis link.
steps: Collect cholestasis symptoms and cholangitis screen.
Discuss differential diagnoses for obstructive jaundice (gallstone obstruction, pancreatic adenocarcinoma, cholangiocarcinoma, benign strictures) and why PPoma is favored given provided context.
Explain diagnosis using required terms (PPoma, cholestasis) and provide safety-net advice.
Record PPoma with cholestasis as the diagnosis.

acceptable_variants.description: Includes brief future management counseling (without prescribing/ordering) mentioning octreotide and ERCP as potential treatments after confirmation or if obstruction requires intervention.
steps: Collect key history for cholestasis and red flags.
Explain PPoma and its association with cholestasis.

Mention that management may include octreotide for symptom/tumor control and ERCP for biliary decompression if obstruction is present, emphasizing these depend on confirmation and specialist evaluation.
Record PPoma with cholestasis as the diagnosis and provide follow-up/safety-net narrative.

patient.chief_complaint: My eyesight has been getting worse, and I keep bumping into things on the sides.

volunteer: My eyesight has been getting worse over the last few months, especially when reading or watching TV.
It feels like my side vision is missing—I don’t notice things until they’re right next to me.
I’ve been getting dull headaches behind my eyes a few times a week.
if_asked: Bright lights bother me more than they used to.

I’ve felt more tired than usual, even after a full night’s sleep.
I’ve had a few episodes of nausea with the headaches, but I haven’t really thrown up.
I’ve noticed I’m thirstier than usual and pee a bit more often.
hidden: My sex drive has dropped a lot, but I figured it was just stress.
My periods have become irregular and lighter than before (or I’ve skipped a couple), which I thought was just hormones/age.
I’ve gained a little weight without changing my eating much.
I feel colder than other people in the same room lately.

misleading: I thought it was just my glasses prescription being wrong or too much screen time.
I wondered if it’s migraines because the headaches and light sensitivity come together sometimes.
I’ve been worried it could be sinus pressure because it feels like pressure behind my eyes.
noise: My neck and shoulders feel tight from sitting at a computer all day.
I’ve had occasional heartburn after spicy food.
My seasonal allergies have been acting up with sneezing and a runny nose.

general_concerns: I’m worried this is definitely a brain cancer because it’s a ‘tumor’ near the pituitary.
My blurry vision must mean my eyes are getting worse; new glasses should fix it, so it might not be related to the brain.
If I don’t have headaches, it can’t be anything serious in the pituitary area.
I read online that pituitary problems always cause major hormone symptoms, so if my periods and weight are mostly normal, it’s probably not the pituitary.
MRI sounds dangerous because of radiation, and I’m concerned it could make things worse.
economic_concerns: I’m not sure my insurance will cover an MRI and specialist visits, so I’m hoping to avoid testing unless it’s absolutely necessary.
If surgery is mentioned, I’m worried I’ll be out of work for a long time and won’t be able to afford unpaid leave.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old female elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and communicate clearly. Start by saying your eyesight has been getting worse for a few months, especially reading/TV, and you keep bumping into things because your side vision feels missing. Also volunteer dull headaches behind your eyes a few times a week.

If asked about associated symptoms, mention light sensitivity, brief blurry/double vision when tired, fatigue, occasional nausea with headaches (no vomiting), and increased thirst/urination.

Do not volunteer hormonal/sexual symptoms unless specifically asked about periods, libido, weight, or temperature tolerance; then disclose irregular/lighter or skipped periods, low sex drive, mild weight gain, and feeling colder than others.

Act reluctant to mention nipple discharge; only admit it if directly asked about breast/nipple changes, pregnancy, or discharge.

Express misconceptions: you thought it was glasses/screen time, maybe migraines, or sinus pressure.

If MRI/specialist/surgery comes up, raise insurance and time-off-work worries and ask if testing is truly necessary. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

required_args.diagnosis: Pituitary dermoid or epidermoid cyst (sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion) with possible optic chiasm compression

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly identifies pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cysts as the leading diagnosis and recognizes potential optic chiasm involvement.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts (or a close equivalent such as sellar/suprasellar dermoid/epidermoid cyst).
Explains that symptoms can arise from mass effect on the optic chiasm (e.g., visual field loss/bitemporal hemianopsia) and/or pituitary dysfunction.
Uses clear, non-alarmist language, checks patient understanding, and invites questions.
Explains the role of MRI in confirming/characterizing the lesion and assessing optic chiasm involvement.
Explains that definitive management is typically surgery when symptomatic, enlarging, or causing visual/endocrine compromise, and sets expectations about goals/risks at a high level.
Elicits and documents key red-flag symptoms requiring urgent escalation (acute/severe headache, sudden vision loss, diplopia/ophthalmoplegia, altered mental status, fever/meningismus).
Asks about visual symptoms (blurry vision, peripheral vision loss), headache pattern, and endocrine symptoms (amenorrhea/galactorrhea, libido changes, fatigue, polyuria/polydipsia).
Avoids prescribing medications in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if any medication discussion arises, performs required safety checks (allergy_check and contraindication_check) before prescribing.
Does not claim that imaging or surgery is already completed; distinguishes suspected vs confirmed diagnosis pending MRI/neurosurgical evaluation.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pituitary dermoid/epidermoid cysts based on sellar/suprasellar cystic lesion presentation and symptoms suggesting optic chiasm/pituitary compression; explain diagnosis and next diagnostic step (MRI).
must_collect: red flags (sudden vision loss, severe acute headache, altered mental status, fever/meningismus)
steps: Clarify chief complaint and timeline; assess severity and progression of headaches and visual changes.
Screen for optic chiasm compression symptoms (peripheral vision loss/bitemporal field issues) and cranial nerve deficits (diplopia).
Screen for pituitary hormone dysfunction symptoms (hyperprolactinemia features, adrenal/thyroid insufficiency symptoms, diabetes insipidus symptoms).
Identify urgent red flags and advise urgent evaluation if present.
Explain suspected diagnosis using required terms: pituitary dermoid and epidermoid cysts; describe relationship to optic chiasm.
Explain that MRI is the key imaging modality to characterize the lesion and guide management.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis tool.
